# 30-Minute LLM/Copilot Presentation — Plan & Notes

I am giving a 30-min internal tech talk about AI capabilities for use in the saas-company I work at. The audience is 40 sw dev coworkers. We have a github copilot business subscription used mostly in visual studio and vscode and only recently for copilot cli. We also use amp and sourcegraph, sparsely. I aim to give tips about low hanging fruits for using AI and also give a brief overview of how it actually works in eg vscode: how does copilot collect the context, send the request in ask/agent/plan mode, access tools, etc. i’m planning on writing the presentation not as a powerpoint for once, but as a github repo markdown or a confluence page.

## The Plan
**Section 1: No-login demo**
Briefly show what anyone can do with a web LLM without logging in — e.g. have Claude produce code snippets or an inline HTML tool.

**Section 2: GitHub Copilot subscription — useful examples**
- Ask Copilot for a PR review
- In Visual Studio: ask Copilot to investigate an exception
- In VSCode: greenfield new feature
- In VSCode: review a React component against best practices
- In Visual Studio: implement a bugfix requiring heavy domain code understanding
- Show AMP and Sourcegraph as complementary tools
- Copilot CLI
- (More TBD)

**Section 3: Deeper dive into LLMs and configs**
- The most eye-opening / surprising insights
- One slide: how the LLM/transformer works to produce tokens one at a time
- How this impacts workflows in VSCode — usefulness of plan-then-execute
- The context window: what it is and why it matters
- Token usage and cost
- What goes into the context in various situations
- Getting better output by tuning your home-dir claude.md

**Section 4: Explicit non-topics**
Not covering: subagents, team of agents, MCP, RAG, etc.

**Section 5: Getting started and keeping up**
Musing on how to get started and stay current — curated list of useful online resources.

---

## Advisor Comments

**On timing:** 30 minutes is tight for all five sections. Section 2 alone with live demos could eat 25 minutes. Since screenshots are planned (not live demos), more scenarios become feasible. Still: be ruthless about what makes the cut.

**Section 1** is a 2-minute point, not a section. Fold it into an opening hook: "here's what anyone can do right now without a subscription" — then move on. It sets stakes without consuming time.

**Section 2** is the strongest material because it's concrete and immediately applicable. Even with screenshots, the list is long. Suggested cut: keep PR review (high wow factor, zero setup required), exception investigation (relatable pain point), and greenfield feature (shows the full agentic loop). Consider dropping the best-practices review and the bugfix — similar to the greenfield demo and will crowd the time. AMP and Sourcegraph: mention by name with one sentence each rather than dedicating screenshot sequences to them.

**Screenshots vs live demos:** Good choice. Screenshots let you cherry-pick good outputs and control pacing precisely. Stage each scenario as a 2-3 screenshot sequence (problem → model response → result applied) rather than single screenshots of walls of text. A brief verbal note that outputs vary and this represents a good but not unusual result will pre-empt "is this cherry-picked?" skepticism.

**Section 3** is where the unique insight lives. Most "intro to Copilot" talks are pure demos. This section is the differentiator — explaining *why* things work the way they do changes how people actually work. Consider expanding relative to current plan. The one-slide transformer overview is good but keep it ruthlessly simple — one diagram, the key consequence facts, move on. The facts that matter most for practitioners: stateless (no memory between conversations), flat token sequence (no privileged structure), recency dominates (last things said matter most), no guaranteed instruction parsing (it's probability all the way down). Those four explain almost everything about productive LLM use. For visuals in this section: hand-drawn or Keynote diagrams will land better than screenshots of model output — simpler, and yours.

**Section 4** (explicit exclusions): don't put this on a slide. Say it verbally at the start. A slide of things you're not covering draws attention to gaps.

**Section 5** is a good closer — resources land well when people are thinking "how do I continue this." Curate ruthlessly: five genuinely essential resources beats twenty that nobody will read.

**Proposed time split for 30 minutes:**
- Opening hook (incl. no-login point): 3 min
- Screenshot demos (3 focused scenarios): 12 min
- Mental model / surprising insights: 10 min
- Resources and how to keep up: 5 min

```

4. Identifies potential logic errors and edge cases

### Tips for Better Reviews

- **Be specific**: "Review for security issues" > "Review this PR"
- **Provide context**: Mention what the PR is supposed to do
- **Follow up**: Ask about specific concerns Copilot raises

::: notes

The key is being specific in your request. Generic "review this" gives generic results.

:::

---

## Real Impact at Siteimprove

In Q4 2025, our team ran an experiment:

- 50 PRs reviewed with Copilot assistance
- 23 additional issues found beyond human review
- 8 security vulnerabilities caught before production
- Average review time reduced by 12 minutes

### Related Internal Resources

## Scenario 2: Exception Investigation

### Visual Studio

**The pain**: Stack trace from production

```csharp
System.InvalidOperationException: Collection was modified
  at CoreApp.Services.CacheManager.Refresh()
  at CoreApp.Jobs.DataSyncJob.Execute()
```

**The ask**: "Investigate this exception in context of our codebase"

---

## What Copilot Did

- Found the `CacheManager` class
- Identified thread-safety issue in `Refresh()` method
- Suggested a lock-based fix
- Provided example with `ConcurrentDictionary`

**Time saved**: 45 minutes of debugging

::: notes

This scenario shows Copilot's ability to navigate codebases. It doesn't just explain the exception - it finds the relevant code and suggests specific fixes based on our patterns.

:::

---

## Exception Investigation Details

### Before (Broken)

```csharp
public void Refresh() {
    foreach (var key in _cache.Keys) {
        if (IsExpired(key)) {
            _cache.Remove(key);  // Modifies during iteration!
        }
    }
}
```

### After (Fixed)

```csharp
public void Refresh() {
    var expiredKeys = _cache.Keys
        .Where(IsExpired)
        .ToList();
    foreach (var key in expiredKeys) {
        _cache.Remove(key);
    }
}
```

## Greenfield Feature Code

```csharp
public class RateLimitMiddleware {
    private readonly IDistributedCache _cache;
    private readonly RateLimitOptions _options;
    
    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context) {
        var key = GetClientKey(context);
        var requests = await GetRequestCountAsync(key);
        
        if (requests >= _options.MaxRequests) {
            context.Response.StatusCode = 429;
            await context.Response.WriteAsync(
                "Rate limit exceeded");
            return;
        }
        
        await IncrementRequestCountAsync(key);
        await _next(context);
    }
}
```

# 🧠 How It Actually Works

## Mental Models That Matter

::: notes

Let's demystify what's happening under the hood. Understanding these concepts will make you more effective with AI tools.

:::

---

## The Transformer: One Token at a Time

```text
Input:  "function calculate"
Output: "Total" (most probable next token)
        ↓
Input:  "function calculateTotal"  
Output: "(" (most probable next token)
        ↓
Input:  "function calculateTotal("
Output: "items" (most probable next token)
```

**Key insight**: The model doesn't "understand" your code - it predicts the most likely continuation based on patterns in training data.

::: notes

This is the fundamental mechanism. Each word (token) is predicted independently based on what came before. No plan, no understanding - just statistical pattern matching at massive scale.

:::

---

## Why This Matters

### Implications for Your Workflow

- **Recency bias**: Last few messages matter most
- **No memory**: Each conversation starts fresh (unless you provide context)
- **Stateless**: Can't "remember" previous sessions
- **Probabilistic**: Same input ≠ same output

### Practical Takeaway

Give context explicitly. "Fix the bug" won't work.  
"Fix the null reference in UserController.Login() where email validation fails" will.

::: notes

This explains why being specific matters. The model doesn't remember your last conversation or know your codebase structure - it only knows what's in the current context window.

:::

---

## The Context Window

### What It Is

A finite buffer of tokens sent to the model

- **Claude**: Up to 200,000 tokens (~500 pages)
- **GPT-4**: Up to 128,000 tokens (~300 pages)
- **Gemini**: Up to 1,000,000 tokens (experimental)

### What Goes In

- Your prompt
- Open files (VSCode)
- Relevant code snippets
- Chat history
- System instructions

---

## Context Window Implications

### Why It Matters

- **Limited space**: Can't send your entire codebase
- **Smart selection required**: Tools must choose what's relevant
- **Token cost**: Larger context = more expensive requests

### How VSCode/Copilot Handles This

1. Identifies relevant files based on your query
2. Includes currently open/focused files
3. Adds semantic matches from workspace
4. Prioritizes recent conversation context

::: notes

This is why Copilot sometimes "forgets" context or makes mistakes - relevant code might simply not fit in the context window. Understanding this helps you provide better prompts.

:::

---

## Context in Different Modes

### Autocomplete (Inline Suggestions)

- Current file (± 50 lines around cursor)
- Recently opened files
- Imported modules

### Chat Mode

- Your explicit question
- Selected code (if any)
- Current file
- Recent chat messages

### Agent Mode ("Plan & Execute")

- All of the above, plus:
- Related files it discovers
- Tool outputs (file reads, searches)
- Its own generated plan

::: notes

Agent mode can gather more context by using tools. This is why it's more powerful but also slower - it's actively searching your codebase and building up context.

:::

---

## Token Usage and Cost

### What's a Token?

Roughly 4 characters, or ¾ of a word

```text
"function calculateTotal" = ~4 tokens
500 lines of code ≈ 1,500 tokens
```

### Typical Request Costs

- **Autocomplete**: 1k-3k tokens (mostly context)
- **Chat query**: 5k-15k tokens
- **Agent task**: 20k-100k tokens (multiple iterations)

### Why This Matters

Your company pays per token. Efficient prompts save money and get faster responses.

::: notes

Being specific actually saves money - a precise prompt might use 10k tokens and get the right answer, while vague prompts might require 50k tokens across multiple attempts.

:::

---

## Improve Outputs with Configuration

### The `.claude.md` File

Place in your home directory:

```markdown
# Project Context
- C# backend with .NET 8
- React frontend with TypeScript
- Use async/await, never .Result
- Follow our error handling pattern (Result<T>)

# Code Style
- Clear variable names over clever code
- Write XML docs for public APIs
- Unit tests use FluentAssertions
```

**Result**: Copilot generates code matching YOUR patterns

::: notes

This is one of the highest-leverage techniques. A few lines of configuration dramatically improve output quality across all tools. Update it as your patterns evolve.

:::

---

## Example Configuration Impact

### Without Config

```csharp
public User GetUser(int id) {
    return _context.Users.Find(id);
}
```

### With Config Specifying Result\<T\> Pattern

```csharp
public async Task<Result<User>> GetUserAsync(int id) {
    try {
        var user = await _context.Users
            .FindAsync(id);
        return user != null 
            ? Result<User>.Success(user)
            : Result<User>.Failure("User not found");
    }
    catch (Exception ex) {
        return Result<User>.Error(ex);
    }
}
```

---

## Effective Prompting Strategies

### Be Specific

❌ "Add validation"  
✅ "Add email format validation using regex, return 400 with error message if invalid"

### Provide Context

❌ "Fix this bug"  
✅ "This throws NullReferenceException when user.Email is null. Handle gracefully with error message."

### Request Format

❌ "Make it better"  
✅ "Refactor using the strategy pattern. Create IPaymentProcessor interface."

::: notes

Specificity is the key to good results. Treat the AI like a junior developer who is smart but doesn't know your codebase - explicit instructions work best.

:::

---

## When AI Struggles

### Limitations to Remember

- **No real-time data**: Training data has a cutoff date
- **No internet access**: Can't look up current documentation (in most tools)
- **Hallucinations**: Sometimes makes up APIs or facts
- **Context limits**: Can't analyze entire large codebases

### Your Safety Nets

1. Review all generated code
2. Run tests before committing
3. Verify external API calls/libraries exist
4. Check for security issues

::: notes

AI is a powerful assistant, not a replacement for critical thinking. Always verify, especially for security-critical code or external dependencies.

:::

---

## Surprising Insights

### Things That Changed My Mental Model

1. **It's not search**: It generates from patterns, doesn't retrieve facts
2. **Conversation quality compounds**: Good early messages = better later results
3. **The 80/20 rule applies**: Gets you 80% there in 20% of the time
4. **Chain of thought helps**: Asking it to "explain step-by-step" improves accuracy
5. **Temperature matters**: Lower = more predictable, higher = more creative

::: notes

These insights aren't obvious until you've used AI tools extensively. Understanding these helps you avoid common pitfalls and use the tools more effectively.

:::

input.content:
# GitHub Actions Workflow: Build and Deploy Slides

This workflow automatically builds presentations using **mkslides** and deploys them to GitHub Pages.

## How It Works

### Trigger Events

The workflow runs on:
- **Push to `main`**: Builds and deploys all presentations
- **Pull Request to `main`**: Builds all presentations (no deployment)
- **Manual trigger**: Via workflow_dispatch

### Build Process

1. **Find Presentations**: Automatically discovers all directories containing `slides*.md` files
2. **Build Each Presentation**: Runs `mkslides build` for each presentation directory
3. **Create Index Page**: Generates a landing page listing all presentations
4. **Deploy to GitHub Pages**: Publishes the built slides (only on push to main)

## Directory Structure

```
repo-root/
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── build-slides.yml
├── 2026-02-ai-in-siteimprove/
│   ├── slides.md            # Slidev format
│   ├── slides2.md           # reveal-md format
│   ├── slides3.md           # mkslides format ✅
│   └── mkslides.yml         # Optional configuration
└── [future-presentation]/
    ├── slides.md            # Any slides*.md file
    └── mkslides.yml         # Optional configuration
```

## Adding a New Presentation

1. Create a new folder in the repository root (e.g., `2026-03-new-topic/`)
2. Add your presentation as `slides.md`, `slides1.md`, `slides2.md`, etc.
3. (Optional) Add a `mkslides.yml` configuration file
4. Commit and push to main
5. The workflow will automatically build and deploy it!

## Configuration

### Per-Presentation Configuration

Each presentation can have its own `mkslides.yml` file to customize:

- **Theme**: Choose from reveal.js themes (black, white, league, dracula, etc.)
- **Syntax highlighting**: Pick a highlight.js theme (monokai, github, vs, etc.)
- **Layout options**: Slide size, transitions, navigation controls
- **Custom CSS/JS**: Add plugins or custom styling

See the [mkslides documentation](https://pypi.org/project/mkslides/) for all options.

### Example mkslides.yml

```yaml
slides:
  theme: dracula
  highlight_theme: atom-one-dark
  title: "My Awesome Presentation"

revealjs:
  width: 1920
  height: 1080
  transition: fade
  slideNumber: 'c/t'
```

## Viewing the Presentations

After deployment, presentations are available at:

- **Index**: `https://[your-username].github.io/[repo-name]/`
- **Individual presentations**: `https://[your-username].github.io/[repo-name]/[folder-name]/`

## Best Practices

### Markdown Format

mkslides uses standard markdown with:

- `---` to separate horizontal slides
- `----` to separate vertical slides (nested)
- `::: notes` blocks for speaker notes

### Example Slide

```markdown
---
title: My Presentation
theme: black
---

# Main Title

Welcome to my presentation

::: notes
These are speaker notes that won't appear on the slide.
:::

---

## Second Slide

Content here
```

### Front Matter

Add YAML front matter at the top of your markdown file to override configuration:

```markdown
---
slides:
  theme: dracula
  highlight_theme: vs
revealjs:
  transition: zoom
---

# Your Presentation Starts Here
```

## Troubleshooting

### Presentation Not Building

- Ensure your file is named `slides*.md` (e.g., `slides.md`, `slides1.md`, `slides3.md`)
- Check the Actions tab for build errors
- Verify your `mkslides.yml` syntax is valid YAML

### GitHub Pages Not Updating

- Ensure GitHub Pages is enabled in repository settings
- Go to Settings → Pages → Source should be "GitHub Actions"
- Wait a few minutes for deployment to complete

### Slides Look Wrong

- Check your `mkslides.yml` configuration
- Try a different theme
- Verify CSS/JavaScript plugins are loading correctly

## Resources

- [mkslides Documentation](https://pypi.org/project/mkslides/)
- [Reveal.js Themes](https://revealjs.com/themes/)
- [Highlight.js Themes](https://highlightjs.org/examples)
- [Reveal.js Configuration](https://revealjs.com/config/)

# Tech Talks

This repository hosts internal tech talk presentations. Each presentation lives in its own folder and is automatically built and deployed via GitHub Actions.

See [#dev_techtalk](https://siteimprove.slack.com/archives/C5KQ35ABX)

## 🎯 Features

- **Automatic Building**: Presentations are automatically built using mkslides when merged to main
- **GitHub Pages Deployment**: All presentations are deployed to GitHub Pages with an index landing page
- **Multiple Formats Supported**: Each presentation folder can contain multiple slide formats (Slidev, reveal-md, mkslides)
- **Customizable Styling**: Each presentation can have its own `mkslides.yml` configuration

## 📂 Repository Structure

```
dev-tech-talk/
├── .github/
│   ├── workflows/
│   │   └── build-slides.yml     # Automated build & deploy
│   └── README.md                # Workflow documentation
├── 2026-02-ai-in-siteimprove/
│   ├── slides.md                # Slidev format
│   ├── slides2.md               # reveal-md format
│   ├── slides3.md               # mkslides format ✅
│   ├── mkslides.yml             # Configuration
│   └── ...
└── README.md                    # This file
```

## 🚀 Adding a New Presentation

1. **Create a new folder** for your presentation:
   ```bash
   mkdir YYYY-MM-topic-name
   cd YYYY-MM-topic-name
   ```

2. **Create your slides** as `slides.md` (or `slides1.md`, `slides2.md`, etc.):
   ```markdown
   ---
   title: My Awesome Presentation
   ---
   
   # My Awesome Presentation
   
   Your content here
   
   ---
   
   ## Second Slide
   
   More content
   ```

3. **(Optional) Add configuration** as `mkslides.yml`:
   ```yaml
   slides:
     theme: dracula
     highlight_theme: monokai
   
   revealjs:
     transition: fade
   ```

4. **Commit and push**:
   ```bash
   git add .
   git commit -m "Add new presentation"
   git push origin main
   ```

5. **Done!** The GitHub Action will automatically build and deploy your presentation.

## 🎨 Presentation Format (mkslides)

mkslides uses standard markdown with reveal.js:

- **Horizontal slides**: Separated by `---`
- **Vertical slides**: Separated by `----` (nested under horizontal slides)
- **Speaker notes**: Enclosed in `::: notes` blocks
- **Code blocks**: Use standard markdown code fences with syntax highlighting
- **Front matter**: YAML configuration at the top of the file

### Example

```markdown
---
title: My Presentation
theme: black
---

# Title Slide

Welcome!

::: notes
These notes are only visible in speaker mode.
:::

---

## Content Slide

- Point one
- Point two

```javascript
function example() {
  console.log("Code with syntax highlighting");
}
```

---

## Another Slide

Content continues...
```

## 🎭 Themes and Customization

### Available Themes

Choose from reveal.js built-in themes:
- `black` (default) - Black background, white text
- `white` - White background, black text
- `league` - Gray background (day)
- `beige` - Beige background
- `sky` - Blue gradient background
- `night` - Black background (high contrast)
- `serif` - Cappuccino background, serif fonts
- `simple` - White background, minimal
- `solarized` - Cream background
- `blood` - Dark background, blood red accents
- `moon` - Dark purple/gray
- `dracula` - Dracula color scheme

### Code Highlighting Themes

Popular options:
- `monokai` - Dark, Sublime Text style
- `github` - GitHub style
- `vs` - Visual Studio style
- `atom-one-dark` - Atom editor dark theme
- `dracula` - Dracula theme
- `tokyo-night-dark` - Tokyo Night dark theme

## 🌐 Viewing Presentations

### After Deployment

- **Index page**: `https://[username].github.io/dev-tech-talk/`
- **Individual presentation**: `https://[username].github.io/dev-tech-talk/2026-02-ai-in-siteimprove/`

### Local Development

To preview locally before pushing:

```bash
# Install mkslides
pip install mkslides

# Navigate to your presentation folder
cd 2026-02-ai-in-siteimprove

# Serve with live reload
mkslides serve

# Or build to static files
mkslides build
```

## 📚 Resources

- **mkslides**: [Documentation](https://pypi.org/project/mkslides/) | [GitHub](https://github.com/MartenBE/mkslides)
- **reveal.js**: [Documentation](https://revealjs.com/) | [Themes](https://revealjs.com/themes/)
- **Workflow Details**: [.github/README.md](.github/README.md)

## 🔧 CI/CD Pipeline

The repository uses GitHub Actions to:

1. **Detect** all folders containing `slides*.md` files
2. **Build** each presentation using mkslides
3. **Generate** an index page listing all presentations
4. **Deploy** to GitHub Pages (on push to main)

See [.github/README.md](.github/README.md) for detailed workflow documentation.

## 💡 Tips

- **Keep it simple**: Start with default settings, customize later
- **Test locally**: Use `mkslides serve` to preview before committing
- **Use speaker notes**: Add `::: notes` blocks for presentation guidance
- **Optimize images**: Keep file sizes reasonable for faster loading
- **Version control**: Each talk in its own dated folder for easy organization

--- title: Your Presentation Title author: Your Name date: YYYY-MM-DD --- # Your Presentation Title ## Subtitle or tagline **Your Name — Month Year** ::: notes Welcome notes go here. These are only visible in speaker mode (press 'S' during presentation). Use these notes to remind yourself of: - Key points to emphasize - Stories or examples to share - Timing reminders - Questions to ask the audience ::: --- ## Agenda 1. **Section One**: What we'll cover first 2. **Section Two**: The main content 3. **Section Three**: Wrapping up 4. **Q&A**: Discussion time ::: notes Keep agenda simple - 3-5 main points maximum. Tell them this will take about X minutes. ::: --- # Section One ## Introduction ::: notes Start with a hook - why should they care? ::: --- ## Slide with Bullet Points Key concepts: - **First point**: Brief explanation - Sub-point for context - Another detail - **Second point**: More information - **Third point**: Keep it concise ::: notes Don't read the slides verbatim. Use bullets as talking points and elaborate verbally. ::: --- ## Slide with Code Example in JavaScript: ```javascript function greet(name) { console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`); } greet('World'); ``` The code above demonstrates... ::: notes Walk through the code line by line if needed. Ask if anyone has questions about the syntax. ::: --- ## Multi-Language Code Examples **Python:** ```python def calculate_total(items): return sum(item.price for item in items) ``` **C#:** ```csharp public decimal CalculateTotal(List items) { return items.Sum(item => item.Price); } ``` --- ## Slide with Image ![Description of image](public/images/example.png) *Caption: Brief explanation of what the image shows* ::: notes Reference the image and explain key elements. Point out what they should notice. ::: --- ## Two Column Layout
### Left Column - Point one - Point two - Point three
### Right Column - Different perspective - Comparison - Contrast
::: notes Use two-column layouts sparingly - they can be hard to read from the back of the room. ::: --- ## Emphasized Quote or Callout > "A meaningful quote that supports your point." > > — Attribution **Key Takeaway**: Single most important message from this section. --- # Section Two ## Main Content ::: notes Transition clearly between sections. Remind them where we are in the agenda. ::: --- ## Data or Statistics **Key Metrics:** - 📈 **85%** improvement in performance - ⏱️ **3 seconds** average response time - 🎯 **99.9%** uptime achieved *Source: Internal testing, Q4 2025* ::: notes Have the full data ready if someone asks for details. Be prepared to explain methodology. ::: --- ## Comparison Table | Feature | Option A | Option B | Option C | |---------|----------|----------|----------| | Speed | Fast | Medium | Slow | | Cost | Low | Medium | High | | Quality | Good | Better | Best | **Recommendation**: Choose based on your priorities --- ## Common Pitfalls ❌ **Don't do this:** - Mistake or anti-pattern - Another common error ✅ **Instead, do this:** - Correct approach - Best practice ::: notes Share war stories if you have them - people remember anecdotes better than abstract advice. ::: --- ## Live Demo **What we'll demonstrate:** 1. Step one 2. Step two 3. Expected outcome *Demo time! (or show screenshot if live demo fails)* ::: notes IMPORTANT: Have screenshots as backup! Test the demo before the presentation. Have a coworker ready to help if needed. ::: --- # Section Three ## Wrapping Up ::: notes Signal that we're nearing the end. Start summarizing key points. ::: --- ## Key Takeaways 1. 🎯 **Main Point**: Most important thing to remember 2. 💡 **Insight**: Key learning or realization 3. 🚀 **Action Item**: What to do next --- ## Resources **Documentation:** - [Resource 1](https://example.com) - What it covers - [Resource 2](https://example.com) - Why it's useful **Internal Links:** - Confluence page - GitHub repository - Slack channel ::: notes Make sure all links are accessible to your audience. Consider creating a shortened link or QR code. ::: --- ## Next Steps **This week:** - ✅ Try the basic example - ✅ Read the documentation **This month:** - ✅ Implement in your project - ✅ Share your experience **Stay connected:** #your-slack-channel --- # Questions? ## Let's Discuss **Contact:** - 💬 Slack: @yourhandle - 📧 Email: your.email@company.com - 💻 GitHub: @yourusername ::: notes Open the floor for questions. Be prepared for common questions about: - [Anticipated question 1] - [Anticipated question 2] - [Anticipated question 3] ::: --- # Thank You! ## [Memorable closing statement] **Slides available at:** `github.com/your-org/repo` ::: notes Thank everyone for their time and attention. Remind them where to find the slides. Stick around for follow-up questions. :::

tools.name: ask_questions
tools.description:
Ask the user questions to clarify intent, validate assumptions, or choose between implementation approaches. Prefer proposing a sensible default so users can confirm quickly.

Only use this tool when the user's answer provides information you cannot determine or reasonably assume yourself. This tool is for gathering information, not for reporting status or problems. If a question has an obvious best answer, take that action instead of asking.

When to use:
- Clarify ambiguous requirements before proceeding
- Get user preferences on implementation choices
- Confirm decisions that meaningfully affect outcome

When NOT to use:
- The answer is determinable from code or context
- Asking for permission to continue or abort
- Confirming something you can reasonably decide yourself
- Reporting a problem (instead, attempt to resolve it)

Question guidelines:
- NEVER use `recommended` for quizzes or polls. Recommended options are PRE-SELECTED and visible to users, which would reveal answers
- Batch related questions into a single call (max 4 questions, 2-6 options each; omit options for free text input)
- Provide brief context explaining what is being decided and why
- Only mark an option as `recommended` to suggest YOUR preferred implementation choice
- Keep options mutually exclusive for single-select; use `multiSelect: true` only when choices are additive and phrase the question accordingly

After receiving answers:
- Incorporate decisions and continue without re-asking unless requirements change

An "Other" option is automatically shown to users—do not add your own.

questions.description: Array of 1-4 questions to ask the user

header.description: A short label (max 12 chars) displayed as a quick pick header, also used as the unique identifier for the question

question.description: The complete question text to display

options.description: 0-6 options for the user to choose from. If empty or omitted, shows a free text input instead.

allowFreeformInput.description: When true, allows user to enter free-form text in addition to selecting options. Use when the user's opinion or custom input would be valuable.

tools.name: create_directory
tools.description: Create a new directory structure in the workspace. Will recursively create all directories in the path, like mkdir -p. You do not need to use this tool before using create_file, that tool will automatically create the needed directories.

dirPath.description: The absolute path to the directory to create.

tools.name: create_file
tools.description: This is a tool for creating a new file in the workspace. The file will be created with the specified content. The directory will be created if it does not already exist. Never use this tool to edit a file that already exists.

filePath.description: The absolute path to the file to create.

content.description: The content to write to the file.

tools.name: create_new_jupyter_notebook
tools.description: Generates a new Jupyter Notebook (.ipynb) in VS Code. Jupyter Notebooks are interactive documents commonly used for data exploration, analysis, visualization, and combining code with narrative text. Prefer creating plain Python files or similar unless a user explicitly requests creating a new Jupyter Notebook or already has a Jupyter Notebook opened or exists in the workspace.

query.description: The query to use to generate the jupyter notebook. This should be a clear and concise description of the notebook the user wants to create.

tools.name: create_new_workspace
tools.description:
Get comprehensive setup steps to help the user create complete project structures in a VS Code workspace. This tool is designed for full project initialization and scaffolding, not for creating individual files.

When to use this tool:
- User wants to create a new complete project from scratch
- Setting up entire project frameworks (TypeScript projects, React apps, Node.js servers, etc.)
- Initializing Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers with full structure
- Creating VS Code extensions with proper scaffolding
- Setting up Next.js, Vite, or other framework-based projects
- User asks for "new project", "create a workspace", "set up a [framework] project"
- Need to establish complete development environment with dependencies, config files, and folder structure

When NOT to use this tool:
- Creating single files or small code snippets
- Adding individual files to existing projects
- Making modifications to existing codebases
- User asks to "create a file" or "add a component"
- Simple code examples or demonstrations
- Debugging or fixing existing code

This tool provides complete project setup including:
- Folder structure creation
- Package.json and dependency management
- Configuration files (tsconfig, eslint, etc.)
- Initial boilerplate code
- Development environment setup
- Build and run instructions

Use other file creation tools for individual files within existing projects.

query.description: The query to use to generate the new workspace. This should be a clear and concise description of the workspace the user wants to create.

tools.name: edit_notebook_file
tools.description:
This is a tool for editing an existing Notebook file in the workspace. Generate the "explanation" property first.
The system is very smart and can understand how to apply your edits to the notebooks.
When updating the content of an existing cell, ensure newCode preserves whitespace and indentation exactly and does NOT include any code markers such as (...existing code...).

filePath.description: An absolute path to the notebook file to edit, or the URI of a untitled, not yet named, file, such as `untitled:Untitled-1.

cellId.description: Id of the cell that needs to be deleted or edited. Use the value `TOP`, `BOTTOM` when inserting a cell at the top or bottom of the notebook, else provide the id of the cell after which a new cell is to be inserted. Remember, if a cellId is provided and editType=insert, then a cell will be inserted after the cell with the provided cellId.

anyOf.description: The code for the new or existing cell to be edited. Code should not be wrapped within <VSCode.Cell> tags. Do NOT include code markers such as (...existing code...) to indicate existing code.

items.description: The code for the new or existing cell to be edited. Code should not be wrapped within <VSCode.Cell> tags

language.description: The language of the cell. `markdown`, `python`, `javascript`, `julia`, etc.

editType.description:
The operation peformed on the cell, whether `insert`, `delete` or `edit`.
Use the `editType` field to specify the operation: `insert` to add a new cell, `edit` to modify an existing cell's content, and `delete` to remove a cell.

tools.name: fetch_webpage
tools.description: Fetches the main content from a web page. This tool is useful for summarizing or analyzing the content of a webpage. You should use this tool when you think the user is looking for information from a specific webpage.

urls.description: An array of URLs to fetch content from.

query.description: The query to search for in the web page's content. This should be a clear and concise description of the content you want to find.

tools.name: file_search
tools.description:
Search for files in the workspace by glob pattern. This only returns the paths of matching files. Use this tool when you know the exact filename pattern of the files you're searching for. Glob patterns match from the root of the workspace folder. Examples:
- **/*.{js,ts} to match all js/ts files in the workspace.
- src/** to match all files under the top-level src folder.
- **/foo/**/*.js to match all js files under any foo folder in the workspace.

query.description: Search for files with names or paths matching this glob pattern.

maxResults.description: The maximum number of results to return. Do not use this unless necessary, it can slow things down. By default, only some matches are returned. If you use this and don't see what you're looking for, you can try again with a more specific query or a larger maxResults.

tools.name: grep_search
tools.description: Do a fast text search in the workspace. Use this tool when you want to search with an exact string or regex. If you are not sure what words will appear in the workspace, prefer using regex patterns with alternation (|) or character classes to search for multiple potential words at once instead of making separate searches. For example, use 'function|method|procedure' to look for all of those words at once. Use includePattern to search within files matching a specific pattern, or in a specific file, using a relative path. Use 'includeIgnoredFiles' to include files normally ignored by .gitignore, other ignore files, and `files.exclude` and `search.exclude` settings. Warning: using this may cause the search to be slower, only set it when you want to search in ignored folders like node_modules or build outputs. Use this tool when you want to see an overview of a particular file, instead of using read_file many times to look for code within a file.

query.description: The pattern to search for in files in the workspace. Use regex with alternation (e.g., 'word1|word2|word3') or character classes to find multiple potential words in a single search. Be sure to set the isRegexp property properly to declare whether it's a regex or plain text pattern. Is case-insensitive.

isRegexp.description: Whether the pattern is a regex.

includePattern.description: Search files matching this glob pattern. Will be applied to the relative path of files within the workspace. To search recursively inside a folder, use a proper glob pattern like "src/folder/**". Do not use | in includePattern.

maxResults.description: The maximum number of results to return. Do not use this unless necessary, it can slow things down. By default, only some matches are returned. If you use this and don't see what you're looking for, you can try again with a more specific query or a larger maxResults.

includeIgnoredFiles.description: Whether to include files that would normally be ignored according to .gitignore, other ignore files and `files.exclude` and `search.exclude` settings. Warning: using this may cause the search to be slower. Only set it when you want to search in ignored folders like node_modules or build outputs.

Also use this tool after editing a file to validate the change.

If required to read contents of a cell use this to determine the line range of a cells, and then use read_file tool to read a specific line range. Requery this tool if the contents of the notebook change.

tools.name: get_vscode_api
tools.description:
Get comprehensive VS Code API documentation and references for extension development. This tool provides authoritative documentation for VS Code's extensive API surface, including proposed APIs, contribution points, and best practices. Use this tool for understanding complex VS Code API interactions.

When to use this tool:
- User asks about specific VS Code APIs, interfaces, or extension capabilities
- Need documentation for VS Code extension contribution points (commands, views, settings, etc.)
- Questions about proposed APIs and their usage patterns
- Understanding VS Code extension lifecycle, activation events, and packaging
- Best practices for VS Code extension development architecture
- API examples and code patterns for extension features
- Troubleshooting extension-specific issues or API limitations

When NOT to use this tool:
- Creating simple standalone files or scripts unrelated to VS Code extensions
- General programming questions not specific to VS Code extension development
- Questions about using VS Code as an editor (user-facing features)
- Non-extension related development tasks
- File creation or editing that doesn't involve VS Code extension APIs

CRITICAL usage guidelines:
1. Always include specific API names, interfaces, or concepts in your query
2. Mention the extension feature you're trying to implement
3. Include context about proposed vs stable APIs when relevant
4. Reference specific contribution points when asking about extension manifest
5. Be specific about the VS Code version or API version when known

Scope: This tool is for EXTENSION DEVELOPMENT ONLY - building tools that extend VS Code itself, not for general file creation or non-extension programming tasks.

query.description: The query to search vscode documentation for. Should contain all relevant context.

tools.name: github_repo
tools.description: Searches a GitHub repository for relevant source code snippets. Only use this tool if the user is very clearly asking for code snippets from a specific GitHub repository. Do not use this tool for Github repos that the user has open in their workspace.

query.description: The query to search for repo. Should contain all relevant context.

It takes an array of replacement operations and applies them sequentially. Each replacement operation has the same parameters as replace_string_in_file: filePath, oldString, newString, and explanation. This tool is ideal when you need to make multiple edits across different files or multiple edits in the same file. The tool will provide a summary of successful and failed operations.

explanation.description: A brief explanation of what the multi-replace operation will accomplish.

replacements.description: An array of replacement operations to apply sequentially.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the file to edit.

oldString.description: The exact literal text to replace, preferably unescaped. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string is not the exact literal text or does not match exactly, this replacement will fail.

newString.description: The exact literal text to replace `oldString` with, preferably unescaped. Provide the EXACT text. Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic.

tools.name: replace_string_in_file
tools.description:
This is a tool for making edits in an existing file in the workspace. For moving or renaming files, use run in terminal tool with the 'mv' command instead. For larger edits, split them into smaller edits and call the edit tool multiple times to ensure accuracy. Before editing, always ensure you have the context to understand the file's contents and context. To edit a file, provide: 1) filePath (absolute path), 2) oldString (MUST be the exact literal text to replace including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc), and 3) newString (MUST be the exact literal text to replace \`oldString\` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic.). Each use of this tool replaces exactly ONE occurrence of oldString.

CRITICAL for \`oldString\`: Must uniquely identify the single instance to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations, or does not match exactly, the tool will fail. Never use 'Lines 123-456 omitted' from summarized documents or ...existing code... comments in the oldString or newString.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the file to edit.

oldString.description: The exact literal text to replace, preferably unescaped. For single replacements (default), include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. For multiple replacements, specify expected_replacements parameter. If this string is not the exact literal text (i.e. you escaped it) or does not match exactly, the tool will fail.

newString.description: The exact literal text to replace `old_string` with, preferably unescaped. Provide the EXACT text. Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic.

Code cells are ready to run and don't require any pre-processing. If asked to run the first cell in a notebook, you should run the first code cell since markdown cells cannot be executed. NOTE: Avoid executing Markdown cells or providing Markdown cell IDs, as Markdown cells cannot be  executed.

E.g. instead of using `python -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'`, use this tool to build the command `conda run -n <env_name> -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'`. ALWAYS call configure_python_environment before using this tool. IMPORTANT: This tool is only for Python environments (venv, virtualenv, conda, pipenv, poetry, pyenv, pixi, or any other Python environment manager). tools.name: manage_todo_list
tools.description:
Manage a structured todo list to track progress and plan tasks throughout your coding session. Use this tool VERY frequently to ensure task visibility and proper planning.

When to use this tool:
- Complex multi-step work requiring planning and tracking
- When user provides multiple tasks or requests (numbered/comma-separated)
- After receiving new instructions that require multiple steps
- BEFORE starting work on any todo (mark as in-progress)
- IMMEDIATELY after completing each todo (mark completed individually)
- When breaking down larger tasks into smaller actionable steps
- To give users visibility into your progress and planning

When NOT to use:
- Single, trivial tasks that can be completed in one step
- Purely conversational/informational requests
- When just reading files or performing simple searches

CRITICAL workflow:
1. Plan tasks by writing todo list with specific, actionable items
2. Mark ONE todo as in-progress before starting work
3. Complete the work for that specific todo
4. Mark that todo as completed IMMEDIATELY
5. Move to next todo and repeat

Todo states:
- not-started: Todo not yet begun
- in-progress: Currently working (limit ONE at a time)
- completed: Finished successfully

IMPORTANT: Mark todos completed as soon as they are done. Do not batch completions.

todoList.description: Complete array of all todo items. Must include ALL items - both existing and new.

id.description: Unique identifier for the todo. Use sequential numbers starting from 1.

title.description: Concise action-oriented todo label (3-7 words). Displayed in UI.

status.description: not-started: Not begun | in-progress: Currently working (max 1) | completed: Fully finished with no blockers

tools.name: mcp_pylance_mcp_s_pylanceInvokeRefactoring
tools.description:
Apply automated code refactoring to Python files. Returns refactored content (does not modify original file) unless mode is "update". Use for: extracting functions, organizing imports, improving code structure, applying refactoring patterns.  Optional "mode" parameter: "update" updates the file, "edits" returns a WorkspaceEdit, "string" returns updated content as string. If mode is not specified, "update" will be used as the default. The "edits" mode is helpful for determining if a file needs changes (for example, to remove unused imports or fix import formatting) without making any modifications; if no changes are needed, the result will be either an empty WorkspaceEdit or a message indicating that no text edits were found. Available refactorings: source.unusedImports: - Removes all unused import statements from a Python file. Use when imports are imported but never referenced in the code. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to a Python file with unused imports.
source.convertImportFormat: - Converts import statements between absolute and relative formats according to python.analysis.importFormat setting. Use when import format consistency is needed. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to a Python file with imports to convert.
source.convertImportStar: - Converts all wildcard imports (from module import *) to explicit imports listing all imported symbols. Use when explicit imports are preferred for better code clarity and IDE support. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to a Python file with wildcard imports.
source.convertImportToModule: - Converts `from module import name1, name2` (including wildcard imports) into `import module` and rewrites references to `module.name1`, `module.name2`. Use when you want module-qualified references. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to a Python file with `from ... import ...` statements.
source.renameShadowedStdlibImports: - Renames imported user modules that shadow standard library module names (e.g. a local calendar.py). Use to avoid import shadowing bugs. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to the importing Python file.
source.addTypeAnnotation: - Adds type annotations to all variables and functions in a Python file that can be inferred from their usage. Use when type hints are needed for better type checking and code clarity. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to a Python file with unannotated variables or functions.
source.fixAll.pylance: - Applies all available automatic code fixes from python.analysis.fixAll setting. Use when multiple code issues need to be addressed simultaneously. Requires fileUri parameter pointing to a Python file with fixable issues.

fileUri.description: The uri of the file to invoke the refactoring.

name.description: The name of the refactoring to invoke. This must be one of these [source.unusedImports, source.convertImportFormat, source.convertImportStar, source.convertImportToModule, source.renameShadowedStdlibImports, source.addTypeAnnotation, source.fixAll.pylance]

mode.description: Determines the output mode: "update" updates the file directly, "edits" returns a WorkspaceEdit, "string" returns the updated content as a string. If omitted, "update" will be used as the default. The "edits" mode is especially useful for checking if any changes are needed (such as unused imports or import formatting issues) without modifying the file, as it will return a WorkspaceEdit only if edits are required.

tools.description: Execute Python code snippets directly in the workspace environment. PREFERRED over terminal commands for running Python code. This tool automatically uses the correct Python interpreter configured for the workspace, eliminates shell escaping/quoting problems that plague terminal execution, and provides clean, properly formatted output with stdout/stderr correctly interleaved. Ideal for: testing code, running quick scripts, validating Python expressions, checking imports, and any Python execution within the workspace context. No temporary files needed - code runs directly in memory.

tools.name: run_in_terminal
tools.description:
This tool allows you to execute PowerShell commands in a persistent terminal session, preserving environment variables, working directory, and other context across multiple commands.

Command Execution:
- Prefer ; when chaining commands on one line
- Prefer pipelines | for object-based data flow
- Never create a sub-shell (eg. powershell -c "command") unless explicitly asked

Directory Management:
- Prefer relative paths when navigating directories, only use absolute when the path is far away or the current cwd is not expected
- Remember when isBackground=false is specified, that the shell and cwd are reused until it is moved to the background
- Use $PWD or Get-Location for current directory
- Use Push-Location/Pop-Location for directory stack

Program Execution:
- Supports .NET, Python, Node.js, and other executables
- Install modules via Install-Module, Install-Package
- Use Get-Command to verify cmdlet/function availability

Background Processes:
- For long-running tasks (e.g., servers), set isBackground=true
- Returns a terminal ID for checking status and runtime later
- Use Start-Job for background PowerShell jobs

Output Management:
- Output is automatically truncated if longer than 60KB to prevent context overflow
- Use Select-Object, Where-Object, Format-Table to filter output
- Use -First/-Last parameters to limit results
- For pager commands, add | Out-String or | Format-List

Best Practices:
- Use proper cmdlet names instead of aliases in scripts
- Quote paths with spaces: "C:\Path With Spaces"
- Prefer PowerShell cmdlets over external commands when available
- Prefer idiomatic PowerShell like Get-ChildItem instead of dir or ls for file listings
- Use Test-Path to check file/directory existence
- Be specific with Select-Object properties to avoid excessive output
- Avoid printing credentials unless absolutely required

command.description: The command to run in the terminal.

explanation.description: A one-sentence description of what the command does. This will be shown to the user before the command is run.

goal.description: A short description of the goal or purpose of the command (e.g., "Install dependencies", "Start development server").

isBackground.description:
Whether the command starts a background process.

- If true, a new shell will be spawned where the cwd is the workspace directory and will run asynchronously in the background and you will not see the output.

- If false, a single shell is shared between all non-background terminals where the cwd starts at the workspace directory and is remembered until that terminal is moved to the background, the tool call will block on the command finishing and only then you will get the output.

Examples of background processes: building in watch mode, starting a server. You can check the output of a background process later on by using get_terminal_output.

timeout.description: An optional timeout in milliseconds. When provided, the tool will stop tracking the command after this duration and return the output collected so far. Be conservative with the timeout duration, give enough time that the command would complete on a low-end machine. Use 0 for no timeout. If it's not clear how long the command will take then use 0 to avoid prematurely terminating it, never guess too low.

tools.name: runSubagent
tools.description:
Launch a new agent to handle complex, multi-step tasks autonomously. This tool is good at researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries, use this agent to perform the search for you.

- Agents do not run async or in the background, you will wait for the agent's result.
- When the agent is done, it will return a single message back to you. The result returned by the agent is not visible to the user. To show the user the result, you should send a text message back to the user with a concise summary of the result.
- Each agent invocation is stateless. You will not be able to send additional messages to the agent, nor will the agent be able to communicate with you outside of its final report. Therefore, your prompt should contain a highly detailed task description for the agent to perform autonomously and you should specify exactly what information the agent should return back to you in its final and only message to you.
- The agent's outputs should generally be trusted
- Clearly tell the agent whether you expect it to write code or just to do research (search, file reads, web fetches, etc.), since it is not aware of the user's intent

prompt.description: A detailed description of the task for the agent to perform

description.description: A short (3-5 word) description of the task

tools.name: search_subagent
tools.description:
Launch an iterative search-focused subagent that orchestrates semantic_search, grep_search, file_search and read_file tool calls to explore and gather relevant workspace code for a natural language query.
 CALL THIS FOR ANY TASK THAT REQUIRES CODEBASE EXPLORATION!

Remember:
1. Perform tool calls in parallel whenever possible
2. Avoid redundant calls -- don't repeat identical searches.
3. Stop once you have high-confidence, non-duplicative snippets covering the query scope.

Returns: A list of relevant files/snippet locations in the workspace.

Input fields:
- query: Natural language description of what to search for.
- description: Short user-visible invocation message. 
- details: 2-3 sentences detailing the objective of the search agent.

query.description: Natural language description of what to search for.

description.description: A short (3-5 word) description of the task.

details.description: A more detailed description of the objective for the search subagent. This helps the sub-agent remain on task and understand its purpose.
